nativeRange.habitat: Peat swamp forests and associated blackwater streams; shaded, slow to moderately flowing acidic water stained by organic decay, with dense riparian vegetation and leaf litter

decorPreferences: dense planting reaching the water surface
driftwood and twisted roots for structure
dried leaf litter (Indian almond leaves, oak leaves) for tannins

diet.notes: Micropredator in nature, feeding on small invertebrates, crustaceans, and insect larvae. In the aquarium, accepts frozen and live bloodworm, Tubifex, Daphnia, brine shrimp, and small sinking pellets. Foods must suspend in mid-water — this species does not feed from the surface or bottom. Varied diet is essential; may prey on very small fish tankmates. Larger individuals are prone to hunting nano fish.

avoidWith: large or boisterous fish that outcompete for food
very small nano fish (risk of predation by adults)

summary: The Striped Glass Catfish is a larger, more boldly marked relative of the popular Glass Catfish (K. vitreolus), reaching ~15 cm and displaying a translucent body with a prominent dark lateral stripe. A schooling species from Southeast Asian peat swamps, it requires soft, acidic blackwater conditions and groups of 5 or more.
careNotes: Kryptopterus macrocephalus is a mid-water schooling catfish from the peat swamp forests and blackwater streams of southern Thailand, Peninsular Malaysia, Singapore, Sumatra, Borneo, and Java. It is the larger cousin of the commonly kept Glass Catfish (K. vitreolus), reaching up to 15 cm in length, and is distinguishable by a prominent dark horizontal stripe running along the flank. Like all Kryptopterus catfish, it is scaleless, making it highly sensitive to copper-based medications — these must never be used in tanks housing this species. A school of at least 5 individuals (preferably 6+) is essential; solitary or small-group specimens become stressed and refuse food. A tank of at least 160 L with a 120 cm footprint is recommended to give a school adequate swimming space and to maintain stable water quality. Water conditions should replicate the acidic, very soft blackwater of peat swamps: pH 4.0–7.0, gH 1–10, temperature 20–26°C. Indian almond (Catappa) leaves, peat filtration, or driftwood help achieve the amber-coloured, tannin-rich water the species prefers. Dim lighting, provided by floating plants or tall stem plants, is important — this species is shy under bright conditions and will spend most of its time hiding. A moderate, consistent water flow (4–5× tank volume per hour) is appreciated. The fish positions itself at an angle in the current with its tail pointing down, a characteristic resting posture. It is a mid-water feeder and will not scavenge from the substrate; foods must be offered in suspension (frozen bloodworm, Daphnia, Tubifex, small sinking pellets that drift down slowly). Tank-raised or long-term captive specimens may be weaned onto high-quality frozen and prepared foods more readily. Avoid housing with boisterous, fast-moving, or fin-nipping species that cause stress; similarly, avoid very small fish that may be predated by adult specimens. Copper-based treatments and sudden parameter shifts are the primary husbandry risks. The species is listed as Near Threatened (IUCN) due to peat swamp habitat loss, and is uncommon in the aquarium trade; wild-caught individuals may require acclimatisation to typical aquarium water parameters gradually.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity has not been recorded. Sexual dimorphism is subtle: males are noticeably slimmer and have serrated edges on the pectoral-fin spine, while females are deeper-bodied, particularly when gravid. Spawning behaviour and egg care are unknown from captive reports.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Kryptopterus macrocephalus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (~95–100 mm SL), temperature (20–26°C), pH (4.0–7.0), hardness (18–143 ppm ≈ ~1–10 gH), minimum tank dimensions (120 × 30 cm base), diet (sinking/frozen foods, bloodworm, Tubifex), behaviour (schooling, timid, mid-water feeder), native range (S. Thailand, Peninsular Malaysia, Singapore, Sumatra, Borneo, Java), habitat (peat swamp blackwater), breeding (unrecorded in captivity), and sexual dimorphism (male serrated pectoral spine). Minimum group size listed as 4+; bumped to 5 per task brief.

additional.name: FishBase — Kryptopterus macrocephalus
additional.notes: Cross-checked max size (9.7 cm SL males/unsexed), temperature (22–26°C), pH (6.5–7.4), hardness (10–20 dH), native range (Indonesia, Malaysia, Brunei Darussalam), habitat (peat, shady running waters), taxonomy (Siluridae, Siluriformes), diet (omnivore, larger individuals prey on smaller fish), trophic level 4.2. IUCN Near Threatened noted. FishBase pH 6.5–7.4 is a narrower trade range vs. SeriouslyFish wild range 4.0–7.0; used SeriouslyFish broader range as primary.

additional.name: Diszhal.info — Kryptopterus macrocephalus
additional.notes: Confirmed size (9–10 cm), temperature (22–27°C), pH (4–7), hardness (0–10 NK°), minimum tank 90 L (120 L for 5–6 fish), lifespan (6–8 years), diet (omnivore accepting live/frozen/dried), behaviour (peaceful schooling fish), native range (Brunei, Malaysia, Indonesia), breeding (not bred in aquarium). Tail-down resting posture noted.

additional.name: Scotcat — Kryptopterus macrocephalus
additional.notes: Confirmed size (10 cm), temperature (22–26°C), pH (6.5–7.5), native range (Peninsular Thailand, Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, Borneo), habitat (peat habitats, shady running water), characteristic tail-down stationary posture, noted as less peaceful than K. bicirrhis and uncommon in trade.

additional.name: PetMeDaily — Striped Glass Catfish Care
additional.notes: Trade care guide. Confirmed schooling requirement (groups of 6+), temperature 24–28°C, pH 6.0–7.5, tank 20 gal+, diet (frozen bloodworm, brine shrimp, tropical flakes, sinking pellets), compatible tankmates (small tetras, rasboras, Corydoras), copper/water chemistry sensitivity noted, escape risk noted (tank cover required). Adult size cited as ~4 inches.

fish.breedingNotes: No captive breeding on record. Males are slimmer with serrated pectoral-fin spines; females are deeper-bodied when gravid. Spawning triggers and egg care are entirely unknown from aquarium reports.
